VM scale sets consistently use user-assigned managed identities (100%): because microsoft.compute/virtualmachinescalesets identity is UserAssigned for all instances, plan to provision and attach user-assigned identities to each of the three VM scale sets so they can reuse tenant-managed credentials for Key Vault, logging, and other resource access.

VM scale sets are primarily Windows and sized to E/D series (88% Windows, 52% Standard_E4s_v4 / 32% Standard_D8s_v4): because the virtualmachineprofile shows Windows OS and these SKUs are the most common, favor Windows images and Standard_E4s_v4 or Standard_D8s_v4 VM sizes for the legacy app unless a Linux requirement exists to align with existing capacity and performance patterns.

VM scale sets commonly configure platform fault domains of 5 (51.7%) or 1 (48.3%): because platformfaultdomaincount is split between 5 and 1, decide whether the deployment region supports multiple fault domains and prefer platformFaultDomainCount=5 for better VM distribution where available, or match the tenant's alternate pattern if constraints require 1.

Application Gateway usage is standardized on WAF_v2 with user-assigned identity and HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect: because microsoft.network/applicationgateways shows sku WAF_v2 and identity userAssigned and redirectconfigurations include httpToHttpsRedirect, deploy App Gateway WAF_v2, assign a user-assigned managed identity, and enable an HTTP->HTTPS redirect to match tenant security and routing conventions.

Application Gateway is commonly paired with VNets, NSGs and Public IPs: because association itemsets link applicationgateways to virtualnetworks, networksecuritygroups and publicipaddresses, place the App Gateway in a dedicated AppGateway subnet within the chosen VNet, provision a Standard public IP, and ensure NSG/network controls and routing align with existing network patterns.

Customer-managed disk-keying is uncommon but key vault usage is mature with purge protection (Key Vault enablepurgeprotection True = 1.0): because there is one diskencryptionset and many key vaults with purge protection and long soft-delete retention, plan to implement disk encryption using a Disk Encryption Set backed by a Key Vault that follows tenant conventions (soft-delete + purge protection) and ensure the required identities can access the vault.

Managed disk SKUs skew to Premium_LRS (63.2%): because microsoft.compute/disks shows Premium_LRS as the majority, provision data disks as Premium_LRS for the legacy scale sets to match tenant storage performance expectations while applying encryption.

Key Vaults predominantly use vault access policies rather than RBAC (enablerbacauthorization False ~60.4%): because most key vaults have enablerbacauthorization set to False, align new Key Vaults with tenant practice by planning for access-policies-based granting of the Disk Encryption Set and managed identities unless organizational policy mandates RBAC.

Public IPs and load-balancing components use Standard SKUs across the tenant (Public IP Standard ~95.9%, Load Balancer Standard = 100%): because public-facing networking resources favor Standard-tier SKUs, allocate Standard public IPs and Standard-tier load balancing/App Gateway resources to ensure compatibility with existing network provisioning and SLA expectations.
